name: Zorya Vechernyaya
description: Zorya Vechernyaya, also known as the Evening Star, is one of the two Zorya sisters in Slavic mythology, the other being Zorya Utrennyaya (the Morning Star). She is the guardian of the evening gates of the celestial palace, where she waits for the sun, Dažbog, to return at the end of his daily journey. After Dažbog finishes his travels across the sky, he is met by Zorya Vechernyaya, who closes the gates behind him and prepares him for his rest. She is often depicted as a beautiful maiden, radiating a gentle, twilight glow. Her role is vital in ensuring the safe passage of the sun and the transition from day to night. Some accounts suggest she is married to either Perun or Dažbog, though the details are often vague and vary between different Slavic regions. She symbolizes the end of the day, peace, rest, and the cyclical nature of time. While not as prominently featured as other major Slavic deities, her presence highlights the importance of the celestial order and the balance between light and darkness. The Zoryas are often invoked for protection, especially during twilight hours, and are seen as benevolent figures guiding lost souls or offering comfort to those in need. They also represent the eternal cycle of birth, death, and rebirth, mirroring the sun's daily journey.
subtitle: Guardian of the Evening Star

keyMyths.title: The Sun's Journey
keyMyths.description: Zorya Vechernyaya waits at the western gates to receive Dažbog, the sun god, after his daily journey across the sky, ensuring his safe return.

keyMyths.title: Guardian of the Horses
keyMyths.description: Together with her sister, Zorya Vechernyaya tends to the horses of Dažbog, which are said to carry him across the sky each day.

keyMyths.title: Protection at Twilight
keyMyths.description: She provides protection during the vulnerable twilight hours, warding off evil spirits and ensuring a peaceful transition into night.

sources.author: Vladimir Propp
sources.description: Analysis of recurring motifs in Slavic folktales, including the Zoryas.
sources.text: Analysis of recurring motifs in Slavic folktales, including the Zoryas.
